title: Pollo alla cacciatore
subtitle: Italian hunter's chicken braised with tomatoes, olives, and anchovies
excerpt: Chicken thighs braised in white wine and tomatoes with olives, capers, anchovies, rosemary, and garlic until the meat is falling-tender and the sauce is deep and savoury-sweet. A central Italian country dish: everything into one pot, nothing wasted.
sourceNotes: Pollo alla cacciatore (hunter-style chicken) is a category of Italian braised chicken dishes that appears throughout central and southern Italy, with significant regional variation. The Lazio, Umbria, and Tuscany versions typically use tomatoes, white wine, olives, capers, and anchovies. The Piedmontese version uses red wine. The 'hunter' in the name refers to the tradition of hunters cooking game birds (and subsequently chicken) with foraged herbs and preserved ingredients carried in the field.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day once the flavours have deepened. Reheat gently.

content.text: The anchovies are the invisible seasoning. Stirred into the hot oil early, they melt completely within 2 minutes and disappear — but they leave behind a deep, savoury intensity that you cannot achieve from salt alone. Nobody will taste fish; they will taste a sauce with unusual depth. This is the same technique used in pasta puttanesca and in French tapenade-enriched braises.

Bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs are essential. The bone releases gelatin into the braise and the skin, once browned, forms a layer that bastes the meat during the 45-minute cook. Boneless thighs will work but produce a leaner, less complex result.

items.prepNote: use sparingly — anchovies and capers add significant salt

content.text: Brown the chicken and build the sauce

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} thighs. Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a wide casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the thighs skin-side down for 6 to 7 minutes until golden. Turn and brown for 3 minutes. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{anchovies}} and cook for 2 minutes, pressing them with a spoon until they dissolve into the oil. Add the {{garlic}} and {{rosemary}} and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{olives-black}}, and {{capers}}. Season with pepper — taste before adding salt.

Return the chicken thighs to the casserole skin-side up. Cover and cook over low heat for 45 minutes until the meat is tender and pulling from the bone. Remove the lid for the last 10 minutes to concentrate the sauce slightly. Discard the rosemary sprigs. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with polenta, crusty bread, or pasta.

items.cause: Salt added in addition to anchovies and capers
items.fix: Anchovies plus capers plus olives is a lot of salt — add no additional salt until you have tasted the finished sauce

items.symptom: Anchovies are visible in the sauce as visible pieces
items.cause: Oil not hot enough when anchovies were added, or not cooked long enough
items.fix: The oil must be medium heat and the anchovies must be pressed and stirred constantly for 2 full minutes until they melt completely

items.symptom: Chicken skin is soft and flabby rather than golden
items.cause: Not enough heat when browning, or lid on during braising covering the skin
items.fix: Brown thoroughly before braising; keep skin-side up during the braise and remove the lid for the last 10 minutes

items.cause: Too much liquid from the wine and tomatoes without reduction
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 15 minutes to reduce the sauce; it should coat the chicken, not pool beneath it

content.text: Cacciatore is the name applied to a dozen distinct Italian braises with the common theme of being hunter's food: rustic, one-pot, built from preserved and foraged ingredients that hunters would carry or find. The central Italian version, with tomatoes, olives, capers, and anchovies, is the one most widely known outside Italy and forms the basis of numerous variations. It is a winter and autumn dish — the season when olives are harvested, tomatoes have been preserved, and the flavours of the ingredients are deep enough to carry the braise.
